summary: Gruber announces that Daring Fireball's full-content RSS feed is now free and available to all readers, having previously been restricted to paying members. He explains the feed sponsorship model, which has been successful with strong demand and measurable ROI for sponsors. He addresses concerns that offering full content via RSS might cannibalize web traffic, but reports that page views have remained essentially stable. He theorizes that most RSS subscribers use feeds as notification systems and still visit the actual website to read content.
key_points: The full-content RSS feed was opened to all readers in August 2007, removing the previous paywall requirement
Feed sponsorship has proven effective, with one sponsor tracking $1,500 in direct sales and calling it their biggest business week ever
Despite fears that full-content feeds would kill page views, traffic remained essentially flat after the switch
Gruber theorizes most RSS subscribers use feeds as notifications rather than as their primary reading interface
The non-CPM ad model (The Deck) made page view drops less critical but still worth monitoring
money_quotes: Many people who are subscribed to the RSS feed just use it as a notification system, and when they see something new has been posted, they load the web site.
I enjoy reading actual web sites.

The small segment of readers who don't visit the site anymore probably weren't visiting the site all that frequently before I offered a full feed anyway, because the whole reason they enjoy reading via feeds is that they don't like reading via regular web pages.
I was able to track about $1,500 of sales directly referred from Daring Fireball. Together with other promotions, it was the biggest week ever for my business.

Page views did drop from July to August after I switched to the full feed, but only slightly. So slightly, in fact, that it might have been more because August is generally the slowest month of the year, traffic-wise.
key_insight: Offering full-content RSS feeds doesn't necessarily cannibalize web traffic because most RSS subscribers use feeds as notification systems and still prefer reading on the actual website.
